refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the acceptance criterion (≥0.999), specifies the measurement scope (bidirectional link), and defines the evaluation period. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to equipment immobilization or unsafe states.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during the evaluation period; calculate availability as (time_link_up / total_evaluation_time). Acceptance: measured availability ≥ customer-defined target (default 0.999). Verification method: automated telemetry logging and post-evaluation statistical analysis.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command link from tele-operator to vehicle.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry link from vehicle to tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link degradation or loss and report status via SafetyAlert.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall implement redundancy or failover mechanisms to achieve target availability.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Single communication path with best-effort reliability (no redundancy)
DP-B: Dual independent communication paths (primary + backup) with automatic failover
DP-C: Multi-path routing with load balancing and dynamic path selection
DP-D: Hybrid approach: primary path with secondary backup and periodic health monitoring
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a hybrid teleoperation link architecture comprising a primary communication path (optimized for latency and throughput) and a secondary backup path (independent network/frequency), with continuous health monitoring and automatic failover triggered when primary link quality degrades below threshold. Failover decision logic shall be deterministic and safety-aware, coordinating with Safety Services to ensure vehicle enters safe state during transition.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation link availability to network infrastructure quality (external dependency on ISP/cellular provider)
DP-D couples failover logic to Safety Services (must coordinate safe state entry during link transition)
DP-D couples health monitoring to Telemetry interface (requires continuous status reporting)
coupling_check.notes: Couplings are acceptable because they reflect real system dependencies. Primary risk is external network dependency; mitigation is customer responsibility (SLA with provider). Secondary risk is failover coordination; mitigation is explicit interface contract with Safety Services.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer has access to two independent communication paths (e.g., cellular + satellite, or dual ISP).
ASSUMPTION: Failover latency (typically 100–500 ms) is acceptable for the operational use case.
RISK: If both paths fail simultaneously, link availability target cannot be met; mitigation requires customer to validate path independence.
RISK: Failover logic complexity increases test burden; mitigation is comprehensive scenario testing and formal verification of state machine.
RISK: Secondary path may have higher latency or lower bandwidth; mitigation is operator training and graceful degradation of functionality.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify with customer the acceptable failover latency and the availability of independent communication paths. If dual paths are not feasible, revise DP to DP-A (single path with SLA-based provider selection) and accept lower availability target, or escalate to project management for scope/schedule trade-off.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end teleoperation link architecture, including command transmission, telemetry reception, link health monitoring, failover logic, and coordination with Safety Services. It abstracts the underlying network infrastructure (primary and backup paths) and presents a unified link-state interface to the Vehicle and Safety Services.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) from tele-operator console; Teleoperation module decodes and forwards to Vehicle.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state (position, sensor readings, link quality metrics) from Vehicle to tele-operator console; Teleoperation module aggregates and formats.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints and operational limits from Safety Services; sends link-state alerts (degradation, failover, loss) to Safety Services for safe-state coordination.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Abstracts primary and backup communication paths; Teleoperation module manages path selection, failover, and health monitoring.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability logic. Containment is high because command/telemetry routing, failover, and health monitoring are core teleoperation concerns. Minor diffusion: link-state alerts must be coordinated with Safety Services (interface contract, not code diffusion).

Safety Services should NOT own link availability logic; it owns safe-state policies and constraints. If link availability logic is placed here, it becomes diffused across safety and communication concerns, violating separation of concerns. SafetyAlert interface is sufficient for coordination.

Vehicle should NOT own teleoperation link logic; it owns vehicle dynamics and local control. Placing link availability here couples vehicle design to communication architecture, creating unnecessary diffusion and maintenance burden.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container but is too coarse-grained for detailed link management. Assigning here would defer architectural clarity to lower levels and increase integration risk.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner because: (1) it encapsulates command/telemetry routing and failover logic as a cohesive concern; (2) it interfaces cleanly with Vehicle (via Tasking/Telemetry), Safety Services (via SafetyAlert), and network infrastructure (via Network Link); (3) it minimizes diffusion by centralizing link-state management; (4) it enables independent testing and evolution of teleoperation architecture without impacting Vehicle or Safety Services.
containment_risks: RISK: Teleoperation module may become a bottleneck if failover logic is overly complex; mitigation is to keep failover state machine simple and deterministic.

RISK: Teleoperation module depends on Safety Services for safe-state coordination; if Safety Services is slow to respond, failover may be delayed; mitigation is to define explicit timeout and escalation policies.

RISK: Network Link interface abstraction may hide path-specific latency or bandwidth characteristics; mitigation is to expose link-quality metrics (latency, packet loss, bandwidth) via Telemetry for operator awareness.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm with Safety Manager and Tele-operator that Teleoperation module ownership is acceptable and that SafetyAlert interface provides sufficient coordination for safe failover. If Safety Services requires tighter coupling (e.g., synchronous failover decision), revise IM boundary to include a shared Failover Coordinator sub-module owned jointly by Teleoperation and Safety Services.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid dual-path with failover) directly addresses FR requirement for ≥0.999 availability. DP-D is traceable to sub-FRs (link establishment, telemetry, degradation detection, redundancy). DP-D is feasible given customer access to dual paths and acceptable failover latency. No decoupling or independence violations detected.

diffusion_risks: Failover logic coordination with Safety Services introduces a cross-module dependency; mitigated by explicit SafetyAlert interface contract.
Network Link abstraction may hide path-specific behavior; mitigated by exposing link-quality metrics via Telemetry.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module containment score is 0.95, indicating strong alignment. Diffusion is minimal (0.05) and localized to interface contracts. No code duplication or scattered logic expected. Ownership is clear and testable.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via automated link-state monitoring and post-evaluation statistical analysis. Acceptance criteria (≥0.999 availability) is measurable and objective. Test scenarios can be designed to exercise failover, link loss, and recovery paths.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns link availability logic; Vehicle owns vehicle dynamics; Safety Services owns safe-state policies. Interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and testable.

recommended_revisions: Obtain customer confirmation of dual-path availability and acceptable failover latency before detailed design.
Define explicit timeout and escalation policies for failover coordination with Safety Services.
Specify link-quality metrics (latency, packet loss, bandwidth) to be exposed via Telemetry for operator situational awareness.
Plan comprehensive failover scenario testing (primary path loss, secondary path loss, simultaneous loss, intermittent degradation).

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (link establishment, telemetry, degradation detection, redundancy). DP-D is sound and well-aligned with Teleoperation module. However, sub-FRs require further decomposition to detail failover logic, health monitoring, and safe-state coordination. Proceeding to next level will clarify implementation details and reduce integration risk.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command link from tele-operator to vehicle.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry link from vehicle to tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link degradation or loss and report status via SafetyAlert.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall implement redundancy or failover mechanisms to achieve target availability.

assumptions: Customer has access to two independent communication paths (e.g., cellular + satellite, or dual ISP).
Failover latency of 100–500 ms is acceptable for the operational use case.
Safety Services can respond to link-state alerts within acceptable timeframe (e.g., <1 second) to coordinate safe-state entry.
Network Link interface abstraction is feasible and does not introduce unacceptable latency overhead.
Tele-operator is trained to handle graceful degradation (e.g., reduced bandwidth, higher latency) during failover.
open_risks: External network dependency: if both paths fail simultaneously, link availability target cannot be met. Mitigation: customer validates path independence and negotiates SLAs with providers.
Failover logic complexity: state machine must be deterministic and safety-aware. Mitigation: formal verification and comprehensive scenario testing.
Failover coordination latency: if Safety Services is slow to respond, failover may be delayed. Mitigation: define explicit timeout and escalation policies.
Link-quality metric exposure: if metrics are not exposed via Telemetry, operator may not be aware of degradation. Mitigation: specify metrics and update frequency.
evidence_needed: Customer confirmation of dual-path availability and acceptable failover latency.
Network provider SLAs for primary and backup paths.
Safety analysis of failover scenarios (primary loss, secondary loss, simultaneous loss, intermittent degradation).
Formal specification of failover state machine and safety-state coordination protocol.
Test plan for failover scenario coverage and link availability measurement methodology.
Operator training plan for graceful degradation and failover awareness.
